docs.cliReference.title: CLI Reference
docs.cliReference.description: Complete reference for every lean-ctx command, flag, and read mode.
docs.cliReference.intro: This is the exhaustive reference for every lean-ctx command. For a quick cheat sheet, see Quick Reference. For analytics and dashboard details, see Analytics Guide.
docs.cliReference.shellCompressionDesc: These commands run shell commands through lean-ctx's 90+ compression patterns. Output is automatically stripped of noise, boilerplate, and redundancy before reaching the LLM.
docs.cliReference.execDesc: Execute any shell command and compress its output. Alias: lean-ctx exec.
docs.cliReference.execNote: The shell hook (installed via lean-ctx init) makes this automatic for common commands — you don't need to prefix them with -c. Use -c explicitly for commands not covered by the shell hook.
docs.cliReference.shellDesc: Start an interactive shell session where all command output is compressed. Wraps your default shell (zsh, bash, or fish).
docs.cliReference.rawModeDesc: Skip compression entirely when you need full, unmodified output. Three ways to activate:
docs.cliReference.fileOpsDesc: Direct CLI access to lean-ctx's file reading, searching, and navigation tools. These are the same operations the MCP server provides to AI tools.
docs.cliReference.readDesc: Read a file with optional compression mode. Default mode is full.
docs.cliReference.diffDesc: Compressed diff between two files with structured change summaries.
docs.cliReference.grepDesc: Search file contents with compressed, grouped results. Uses ripgrep internally.
docs.cliReference.findDesc: Find files by name pattern. Respects .gitignore and outputs a compact file tree.
docs.cliReference.lsDesc: Compact directory listing as a tree map with file counts.
docs.cliReference.depsDesc: Show project dependency graph. Detects package manager and extracts dependencies.
docs.cliReference.readModesDesc: Read modes control how lean-ctx read and the MCP tool ctx_read compress file content. Choose the right mode for your use case:
docs.cliReference.modeFullUse: Files you will edit. Re-reads cost ~13 tokens (cached).
docs.cliReference.modeMapUse: Context-only files — understand structure without reading everything.
docs.cliReference.modeDiffUse: After editing — see only what changed.
docs.cliReference.modeLinesUse: Read only the section you need. Supports multiple ranges: lines:10-50,80-100
docs.cliReference.modesSupportedLangs: Supported languages for signatures mode: TypeScript, JavaScript, Rust, Python, Go, Java, C, C++, Ruby, C#, Kotlin, Swift, PHP (14 total via tree-sitter).
docs.cliReference.setupDesc: One-command setup. Auto-detects your shell (zsh/bash/fish/PowerShell), finds installed AI tools, and configures MCP server + shell hooks. Run once after installing lean-ctx.
docs.cliReference.setupStep2: Configures MCP for each detected AI tool
docs.cliReference.initDesc: Install shell compression aliases into your shell profile.
docs.cliReference.initNote: This writes a block to ~/.zshrc, ~/.bashrc, ~/.config/fish/config.fish, or your PowerShell profile. The block includes aliases for git, docker, npm, cargo, and 90+ other commands.
docs.cliReference.initAgentDesc: Configure MCP for a specific AI tool without running full setup.
docs.cliReference.configDesc: Show or edit configuration stored at ~/.lean-ctx/config.toml.
docs.cliReference.updateDesc: Self-update the lean-ctx binary from GitHub Releases.
docs.cliReference.uninstallDesc: Remove all lean-ctx configuration: shell hooks, MCP configs from all editors, and the ~/.lean-ctx/ data directory. Prints instructions for removing the binary.
docs.cliReference.analyticsIntro: For a detailed guide with screenshots and interpretations, see Analytics Guide.
docs.cliReference.gainDesc: Visual terminal dashboard showing lifetime token savings.
docs.cliReference.gainNoneDesc: Full dashboard: total saved, rate, USD, top commands, sparklines
docs.cliReference.gainLiveDesc: Auto-refreshing mode (updates every 2s). Press <kbd>q</kbd> to quit.
docs.cliReference.gainDailyDesc: Bordered day-by-day table with token counts and USD
docs.cliReference.gainJsonDesc: Raw JSON export of all stats (for scripting/automation)
docs.cliReference.cepDesc: CEP (Cognitive Efficiency Protocol) impact report showing score trends, cache hit rates, and mode distribution.
docs.cliReference.dashboardDesc: Open the interactive web dashboard at http://localhost:3333. Features interactive charts, session history, active agents, and project knowledge panels.
docs.cliReference.wrappedDesc: Generate a shareable "Wrapped" report card of your token savings.
docs.cliReference.sessionsMgmtDesc: Manage CCP (Context Continuity Protocol) sessions stored at ~/.lean-ctx/sessions/.
docs.cliReference.sessionAdoptionDesc: Show adoption statistics — how much of your workflow uses lean-ctx compression.
docs.cliReference.benchmarkRunDesc: Run real token benchmarks on your project files using tiktoken (o200k_base). Measures exact savings for each read mode across every file.
docs.cliReference.benchmarkReportDesc: Generate a shareable Markdown report from benchmark results.
docs.cliReference.teeDesc: Manage full output tee files saved to ~/.lean-ctx/tee/. Use lean-ctx tee last to view the most recent saved output. Configure via tee_mode: always, failures, or never.
docs.cliReference.discoverDesc: Scan your shell history for commands that could benefit from compression but aren't being intercepted. Shows per-command token estimates and USD savings projection.
docs.cliReference.cheatsheetDesc: Print a compact cheat sheet of all commands and workflows directly in your terminal.
docs.cliReference.teeManageDesc: Manage error log files stored at ~/.lean-ctx/tee/. When tee_on_error is enabled in config, failed commands log their full output here.
docs.cliReference.slowLogDesc: Show or clear the slow command log at ~/.lean-ctx/slow-commands.log. Commands that take longer than expected are logged here for debugging.
docs.cliReference.shellAliasesDesc: After running lean-ctx init --global, these aliases are available in your shell. They let you toggle compression on and off without restarting:
docs.cliReference.aliasStatusAction: Show whether compression is currently active
docs.cliReference.aliasRawAction: Run a single command without compression
docs.cliReference.whenToDisable: When to disable: Turn off compression when you want to read command output yourself (debugging, reviewing logs). Turn it back on before starting an AI coding session.

manifest.title: Manifest — LeanCTX
manifest.description: A position paper on why information density — not context size — determines AI coding performance.
manifest.heroDesc: A position paper on why information density — not context size — determines AI coding performance. By the LeanCTX team, March 2026.
manifest.abstractQuote: High performance with LLMs isn't about bigger context windows. It's about maximizing information entropy per token. LeanCTX is the intelligence buffer that ensures every token carries maximum signal.
manifest.abstractP1: In 2026, AI coding tools send full files, raw CLI output, and uncompressed project scans into context windows — every single time. The signal-to-noise ratio is abysmal. Based on tool-call analysis in multi-step coding sessions, ~65% of file reads are re-reads. Models waste attention on boilerplate that carries zero information entropy.
manifest.abstractP2: This paper argues that an Intelligence Layer — a transparent compression layer between the developer and the LLM — is the missing piece in the AI engineering stack. We present LeanCTX: a single Rust binary that achieves up to 99% per-operation token reduction (cache re-reads) while preserving all information the model needs to reason correctly.
manifest.problemP1: We have models with million-token context windows and reasoning chains that span hundreds of steps. Yet most AI coding tools still send the full file on every read. That's like sending the entire library every time someone asks for a single page.
manifest.problemP2: The result: diluted attention, wasted compute, and reasoning that loses focus on the logic nodes that actually matter. Every redundant token competes with the actual signal in the attention mechanism — pushing the model's reasoning off the code paths that need analysis.
manifest.problemStat1Note: Based on tool-call patterns in multi-step coding sessions
manifest.problemP3: Every AI tool has hard limits. 500 requests per day. 45 messages per 5 hours. 1,500 premium requests per month. Tokens are the new gold — but most tools burn them on boilerplate with zero information entropy.
manifest.problemP4: The problem isn't the model. It's the input.
manifest.densityP1: A 200K-token context filled with boilerplate produces worse results than 10K tokens of pure signal. This isn't speculation — it's how attention mechanisms work. Every byte of noise stripped is a byte of reasoning capacity gained.
manifest.densityP2: Information entropy — measured in bits per token — is what determines whether a model reasons correctly. High-entropy tokens carry decisions, branching logic, API contracts, error handling. Low-entropy tokens carry whitespace, boilerplate, repetitive imports, and verbose CLI formatting.
manifest.densityHighlightSub: The goal of every Intelligence Layer interaction.
manifest.densityP3: Consider a typical file re-read. The model already knows the file structure, the exports, the types. Sending 3,500 tokens of full source code when a 13-token cache confirmation suffices is a 99.6% waste of context capacity.
manifest.densityP4: The same logic applies to CLI output. npm install generates 800+ tokens of funding notices, deprecation warnings, and formatting. The information content? One line: package name, version, dependency count, timing.
manifest.efficiencyP1: At 80% average compression — achievable with cached reads and shell hook combined — you don't save 80% of cost. You multiply capacity by 5x. Same budget, same subscription, five times the productive output.
manifest.efficiencyStat2Note: Typical session-wide average with caching + shell hook
manifest.efficiencyP2: This isn't about saving money — though it does that too. It's about making every interaction count. Longer sessions without context window resets. Deeper reasoning because the model isn't distracted by noise. Fewer failed completions because the relevant code is actually in the attention window.
manifest.efficiencyP3: The cost curve shifts from linear to logarithmic. Each additional token of context provides diminishing returns when it's noise, but compounding returns when it's signal.
manifest.archP1: LeanCTX implements the Intelligence Layer as four composable layers. Each layer operates independently but compounds when used together.
manifest.archLayer1Desc: AST-based signatures via tree-sitter (18 languages), delta-loading for cached files, session caching with MD5 tracking, entropy filtering via Shannon analysis. Sends the skeleton, not the flesh. Re-reads cost 13 tokens instead of thousands.
manifest.archLayer2Desc: 6 read modes + line ranges let you choose the right fidelity per task. map mode for understanding, full mode for editing, signatures for API surface, entropy for noise filtering.
manifest.archLayer3Desc: Session cache with auto-TTL (5 min idle clear), context checkpoints via ctx_compress, subagent isolation with fresh=true. The model always sees the latest state, not the full history.
manifest.archLayer4Desc: Focused, high-entropy input means sharper reasoning. Less noise in the attention window = more attention on logic nodes = better code output. This is the emergent benefit of all other layers working together.
manifest.archP2: The architecture is hybrid: a context server with 25 intelligent tools that replace editor built-ins (file reads, directory listings, code search, intent detection, project graphs), plus a transparent shell hook that compresses 90+ CLI patterns across 34 categories without changing your workflow.
manifest.shiftP1: The old paradigm sends everything. The new paradigm sends only signal. Here's what changes when you introduce an Intelligence Layer:
manifest.shiftP2: The key insight: this isn't about seeing less. It's about seeing only what matters. The model receives the same logical information — function signatures, dependencies, changed lines, error messages — without the noise that dilutes its reasoning.
manifest.principlesP1: Five principles guide every design decision in LeanCTX:
manifest.principle1Desc: Every compression preserves the information the model needs. AST signatures keep function contracts intact. Diff mode shows exactly what changed. The filter never drops anything critical — every compression reverses cleanly at the semantic level.
manifest.principle2Desc: Every tool reports token counts. ctx_benchmark measures exact savings with tiktoken (o200k_base). ctx_metrics tracks cumulative stats. lean-ctx gain shows lifetime savings with USD cost estimates. You always know what's happening.
manifest.principle3Desc: Single Rust binary. No API keys, no accounts, no telemetry, no data collection. Your code never leaves your machine. MIT licensed, fully open source. Runs on macOS, Linux, and Windows with native binaries.
manifest.principle4Desc: 25 intelligent tools that each do one thing well. Use ctx_read for files, ctx_shell for CLI, ctx_compress for checkpoints. Mix and match for your workflow. Works with Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Windsurf, Codex, and more.
manifest.principle5Desc: All token counts use tiktoken with the o200k_base encoding — the same tokenizer the models use. No approximations, no heuristics. USD cost tracking with persistent lifetime statistics. Data-driven mode selection through ctx_analyze and ctx_benchmark.
manifest.conclusionP1: Token limits, request quotas, and context window sizes define the AI coding landscape in 2026. The path forward isn't bigger context windows — it's making every token carry maximum information entropy.
manifest.conclusionP2: LeanCTX is a lossless minifier for human thought. It doesn't make the model see less. It makes the model see only what matters: the function signatures, the changed lines, the error codes, the dependency graph — stripped of the noise that dilutes reasoning.
manifest.conclusionQuote: 10K tokens of pure signal. That's the future of AI engineering.
manifest.conclusionCta: One Rust binary. Zero cloud dependencies. MIT licensed.

mcpOverhead.title: MCP Wastes 22,945 Tokens Before Your First Message — LeanCTX
mcpOverhead.description: We measured the actual token cost of Model Context Protocol tool definitions in Cursor. The results are alarming: 72% of your context window is consumed before you even ask a question.
mcpOverhead.heroTitle:
MCP Wastes 22,945 Tokens
Before Your First Message
mcpOverhead.heroMeta: March 29, 2026 · 6 min read · by Yves Gugger
mcpOverhead.introP1: We measured the actual token overhead of Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool definitions in a real Cursor session. The results fundamentally challenge the assumption that MCP is efficient for AI-code communication.
mcpOverhead.measurementP1: A standard lean-ctx MCP server exposes 21 tools. Each tool definition includes a name, description, and JSON schema for parameters. We measured the token count of these definitions using tiktoken-rs (the same tokenizer OpenAI uses).
mcpOverhead.stat3Label: per message just for tool overhead (GPT-4)
mcpOverhead.measurementP2: That's 22,945 tokens before the user types a single character. On Claude 3.5 Sonnet at $3/M input tokens, that's $0.069 per message — just for telling the model which tools exist. Over a typical 8-hour coding session with 200 tool calls, that's $13.80 in pure overhead.
mcpOverhead.whyExpensiveTitle: Why MCP Tool Definitions Are So Expensive
mcpOverhead.whyExpensiveP1: MCP was designed for flexibility, not efficiency. Each tool definition contains:
mcpOverhead.whyExpensiveLi1: Verbose descriptions — written for human readability, not token economy
mcpOverhead.whyExpensiveLi2: Full JSON Schema — complete type definitions with descriptions for every parameter
mcpOverhead.whyExpensiveLi3: Redundant information — the same parameter patterns repeated across similar tools
mcpOverhead.whyExpensiveLi4: No compression — sent as raw JSON on every request
mcpOverhead.whyExpensiveP2: For comparison, Cloudflare's "Code Mode" research found similar patterns: AI tools spend the majority of their context budget on protocol overhead rather than actual code understanding.
mcpOverhead.solutionP1: lean-ctx v2.8.2 introduces Unified Tool Mode — consolidating 21 individual tools into 4 core tools + 1 meta-tool:
mcpOverhead.solutionP2: The meta-tool (ctx) acts as a router: ctx(tool="session", action="load") replaces the dedicated ctx_session tool. The 4 high-frequency tools (ctx_read, ctx_shell, ctx_search, ctx_tree) keep their own definitions for zero-overhead access.
mcpOverhead.broaderP1: This isn't just a lean-ctx problem. Every MCP server — GitHub, database tools, cloud providers — faces the same overhead. A typical development environment with 3-4 MCP servers might consume 50,000+ tokens just in tool definitions.
mcpOverhead.broaderP2: The AI-code communication layer needs a new paradigm. Instead of verbose tool schemas, we need:
mcpOverhead.broaderLi1: Compressed tool representations that convey semantics in fewer tokens
mcpOverhead.broaderLi2: Tool consolidation that reduces the number of definitions
mcpOverhead.broaderLi3: Context-aware loading that only sends relevant tools per request
mcpOverhead.broaderLi4: Protocol-level optimization that eliminates redundancy at the wire format
mcpOverhead.broaderP3: This is what lean-ctx is building: not just a compression tool, but an context engineering layer that makes AI-code communication fundamentally more efficient.
mcpOverhead.tryItP1: Run lean-ctx gain to see your actual token savings in real-time.
